title: Beginner Athlete
principles: Keep weekly structure predictable and repeatable
Focus on successful weeks not perfect weeks
Recovery is productive
plan_rules: Default to full-body or upper/lower split
80/20 rule for endurance (no VO2max first 4 weeks)
hard_constraints: No VO2max work first 4 weeks endurance
nutrition_rules: Start with fundamentals: consistent protein, hydration, regular meals
Calorie target + protein target; macros optional
coaching_tone: Encouraging, clear, non-judgmental, educational without being overwhelming
evidence_sources: Helms et al. Muscle & Strength Pyramids
rationale: Beginners benefit most from sustainable structure. Slightly underdosed plans that get completed beat optimal plans that get abandoned.

title: Intermediate Athlete
principles: Progression with structure
Better fatigue management
Goal specificity
More intentional workload distribution
plan_rules: 3-4 week mesocycles with deload every 4th week
nutrition_rules: Goal-specific: Bulk +10-20%, Cut -15-25%, Maintain near TDEE
coaching_tone: Collaborative, specific, slightly more analytical, still practical
rationale: Intermediates benefit from structured periodization and more nuanced programming than beginners.

title: Advanced Athlete
principles: Specificity
Recovery management
Precision
Small changes matter more than dramatic changes
hard_constraints: No aggressive changes without user feedback evidence
coaching_tone: Respectful, precise, less explanatory fluff, more performance-oriented but still grounded
rationale: Advanced athletes need precision programming. Smaller improvements take more specificity and careful recovery management.

title: Age Group 18-29
training_adjustments: Build strong aerobic base and foundational strength
rationale: Young adults have peak recovery capacity and can tolerate higher training volumes while building foundational fitness.

title: Age Group 30-39
rationale: Adults in their 30s begin to see modest recovery changes and benefit from proactive warm-up and structured deloads.

title: Age Group 40-49
nutrition_rules: Recovery protein timing 20-40g within 1-2 hrs post-training
training_adjustments: Joint-friendly alternatives proactively offered (trap bar DL, incline press, goblet squat)
evidence_sources: Phillips et al. 2016 protein with aging
rationale: Athletes in their 40s benefit from joint-friendly alternatives, longer warm-ups, and higher protein intake to support recovery and connective tissue health.

title: Age Group 50-59
nutrition_rules: Protein 1.6-2.2 g/kg default (up to 2.4 g/kg in deficit/heavy training)
training_adjustments: 10-15 min warm-up with joint circles and progressive loading
rationale: Athletes 50-59 need longer warm-ups, bone density emphasis, balance work, and more recovery time between hard sessions.

title: Age Group 60+
nutrition_rules: Protein 1.6-2.2 g/kg default (up to 2.4 g/kg in deficit)
training_adjustments: Resistance training essential 2-3x/week for sarcopenia prevention
coaching_tone: Encouraging, respectful of experience, never patronizing. Focus on what they CAN do.
rationale: Older adults require sarcopenia prevention, fall prevention, functional movements, and careful recovery management with higher protein and micronutrient attention.

title: Male Gender Defaults
training_adjustments: Strength norms from male population data
Upper body emphasis natural but correct imbalances
rationale: Male-specific physiological norms for TDEE calculation, recovery windows, and strength benchmarks.

title: Female Gender Defaults
training_adjustments: Higher fatigue resistance at moderate intensities (more reps at given %1RM)
Menstrual cycle-aware programming opt-in (follicular=higher intensity, luteal=moderate + higher volume -- symptom-driven)
ACL prevention: knee stability, hamstring strengthening
Higher relative recovery for repeated moderate sets
rationale: Female-specific physiological considerations including menstrual cycle awareness, ACL injury prevention, bone density, and iron intake.

title: Gender-Neutral Defaults
nutrition_rules: Activity-adjusted TDEE without sex-specific multipliers (or average)
coaching_tone: Inclusive, non-gendered language. Focus on individual data over population assumptions.
rationale: Gender-neutral approach using bodyweight-relative norms and individual recovery signals rather than sex-specific population data.

title: Strength Training Core
principles: Volume landmarks (Israetel/RP): Large muscles MEV 6-8, MAV 12-18, MRV 20-25+
Small muscles MEV 4-6, MAV 8-12, MRV 15-20
2x/week frequency per muscle optimal
8-12 reps most efficient (stimulus-to-fatigue)
Rest: 3-5 min heavy compounds, 90-120s accessories
plan_rules: Split design: 2-3 days=full body, 3-4=UL or FB, 4-5=UL or PPL, 5-6=PPL
Proactive deload every 4th week (reduce volume 40-50%, intensity 10-15%)
Exercise rotation: Tier 1 stable 4-6 weeks, Tier 2 rotate 2-4 weeks, Tier 3 rotate 1-3 weeks
hard_constraints: Proximity to failure: beginners 3-4 RIR, intermediate 2-3 RIR, advanced 1-2 RIR
rationale: Core strength training module defining volume landmarks, progression models, split design, and deload protocols based on current evidence.

title: Endurance Running Core
principles: Polarized 80/20 (80% Z1-2, 20% Z4-5, minimal Z3)
plan_rules: Volume progression context-based (easy weeks +10-15%, hard weeks +5-8%)
Never add volume AND intensity same week
For runners with a specific time goal: include one weekly interval session (800m or 1K repeats at I-pace). This is non-negotiable for time-goal training.
Total interval volume should be 5-8% of weekly mileage for intermediates, up to 10% for advanced
Place interval sessions on a day following an easy day, never after a long run or tempo
Progress repeats from shorter (400-800m) to longer (1K-1600m) as the race approaches
Reference RUNNING_ZONE_CALCULATIONS module for pacing interval work by HR zones
evidence_sources: Billat et al. — vVO2max interval research
Midgley et al. (2006) — systematic review on VO2max training
rationale: Polarized training distribution and context-dependent volume progression form the foundation of evidence-based running programming. Interval repeats for time-goal runners develop VO2max and race-specific fitness.
change_log: 2026-04-08: Added weekly interval repeat requirement for time-goal runners; linked to zone calculations module

title: 5K Race Plan
plan_rules: Key sessions: 1x tempo 20-40min Z3-Z4, 1x VO2max intervals (5x1000m at 5K pace), 1x progression long run 6-9mi, 1-2x easy runs
rationale: 5K-specific training plan with distance-appropriate key sessions, mileage ranges by level, and short taper protocol.

title: 10K Race Plan
plan_rules: Key sessions: 1x 10K intervals (5x1mi at 10K pace), 1x threshold (2-3x8-10min at LT), 1x progression long 8-12mi, easy runs
rationale: 10K-specific training plan with threshold and interval sessions, progressive long runs, and appropriate mileage ranges.

title: Half Marathon Race Plan
plan_rules: Periodization: Base (wk1-4) aerobic 5-8mi long. Build (wk5-9) tempo+intervals long to 12mi. Peak (wk10-11) race-pace 12-13mi. Taper (wk12+) 2-3 weeks 40-60% reduction
Key sessions: 1x HM pace 10-13mi, 1x tempo 25-35min Z3, 1x 5K/10K-pace intervals, 1x long with HM segments
rationale: Half marathon plan with phased periodization, race-specific long runs, and introduction of race-day nutrition strategy.

title: Marathon Race Plan
plan_rules: Three approaches: Pfitzinger (5-phase, peak 55-85mpw, MP long runs, 3-week taper 70/55/30%), Hansons (cumulative fatigue, 6 days/week, max long 16mi, 3 quality sessions, peak 50-55mpw), Traditional (base/build/peak/taper, long to 18-20mi, 3 quality/week, 3-week taper)
Taper 21 days optimal, volume -41-60%, maintain intensity
Race nutrition: carb load 24-48hr 8-12g/kg, during 60-90g carbs/hr from first 15min
Scale the number of 20+ mile runs by runner experience and weekly mileage: first-timers 0-1, beginners 1-2, intermediates 2-4, advanced 4-5 with 6-12 miles at marathon pace per long run
Never program more than one 20+ mile run per 2-week period
Minimum 10-14 days between 20+ milers for all levels
rationale: Marathon plan offering three proven methodologies with evidence-based taper protocols, experience-scaled long run programming, and critical race-day nutrition strategy.
change_log: 2026-04-08: Added experience-scaled marathon long run programming with safety guardrails

title: Ultramarathon Race Plan
plan_rules: Phases: Base (wk1-8) build to 20mi. Specific (9-16) 20-28+mi with walking. Intensity (17-20) peak 25-32mi. Taper (21-24) progressive reduction
Nutrition: 150-400 kcal/hr, real food OK, practice extensively
rationale: Ultra training prioritizes time-on-feet and nutrition practice over speed, with extended build phases and mandatory cutbacks.

title: Cycling Training
principles: Coggan power zones: Z1 <55% FTP, Z2 56-75%, Z3 76-90% (sweet spot 89-93%), Z4 91-105%, Z5 106-120%
plan_rules: Key workouts: sweet spot 2x20min 90% FTP, VO2max 5x5min 108% FTP, over-unders 90%/105% alternating, endurance 1-4hr Z2
Periodization: Base 8-12wk Z2 to Build 6-8wk Z3-5 to Peak 3-4wk event-specific
rationale: Power-zone-based cycling training with structured periodization from base through peak phases.

title: Swimming Training
plan_rules: CSS-based zones (200m+400m test): Recovery 65-75% CSS, Endurance 75-90%, Threshold 90-100%, VO2max 100-110%
Session structure: warm-up 10-15%, drills 10-15%, main 50-60%, kick/pull 10-20%, cool-down
rationale: CSS-based swimming training with structured session composition and volume progression limits.

title: Triathlon Training
plan_rules: Periodization: traditional or reverse (reverse better for swim)
Concurrent training: min 3hr gap strength/endurance same day, strength first when fresh, max 2 HIIT/week, bike/row for HIIT
rationale: Multi-sport triathlon training with sport-specific volume distribution, brick workouts, and distance-dependent taper protocols.

title: HIIT Training
plan_rules: Formats: circuit (30-45s work, multi-exercise), Tabata (20s/10s x8, advanced only max 1-2x/wk), EMOM, AMRAP
Work:rest ratios: fat loss 1:3 to 1:1, performance 2:1 to 3:1, VO2max 1:1 to 1:2
4-week blocks: wk1-2 build volume, wk3 peak intensity, wk4 deload
nutrition_rules: Pre-HIIT fast carbs 60-90min before (fasted HIIT no fat loss advantage per 2024-2025 reviews)
rationale: HIIT programming with format variety, frequency limits, goal-specific work:rest ratios, and 4-week block structure.

title: Bodyweight Training
plan_rules: Progressive overload without weight: easier-to-harder variations, increased reps/sets (progress 1-2 reps/wk, harder variation at 12-15 reps), decreased rest, increased ROM, eccentric loading (3-5s negatives), tempo manipulation
Cover all patterns 2-3x/week: horizontal push/pull, vertical push/pull, squat, hinge, core, carry
Volume: strength 10-15 sets/wk per pattern, hypertrophy 15-25
evidence_sources: Progressive push-up training comparable to bench press when volume-matched
rationale: Bodyweight training using progressive variation, movement pattern coverage, and evidence showing comparable results to weighted training when volume-matched.

title: Yoga and Mobility
plan_rules: Session: dynamic warm-up 2-3min, main mobility 10-15min (3-4 exercises per region), cool-down 2-3min

Stretching: dynamic pre-workout only (10-12 reps), static post-workout or dedicated days (30-60s hold, 2-4 reps, NEVER before strength), PNF most effective (3-6s contraction then 10-30s stretch 1-2x/wk)
Dynamic pre (5-10min) + static post (10-15min) + 1-2 dedicated days (20-45min)
rationale: Mobility and flexibility programming with evidence-based stretching protocols and timing considerations relative to strength training.

title: Rowing Training
plan_rules: Key workouts: steady state 30-80min Z2, intervals 4x500m threshold, rate work
rationale: HR-based rowing training with steady state and interval key workouts scaled by experience level.

title: Pilates Training
plan_rules: Focus on movement quality not intensity
rationale: Pilates as a complementary modality focusing on core strength and movement quality.

title: Walking
plan_rules: Counts toward daily movement not training load
rationale: Walking as a low-intensity daily movement and active recovery modality, not counted as structured training load.

title: Sport-Specific Performance
rationale: Sport-specific training organized around competition calendars with strength work supporting primary sport demands.

title: Muscle Gain / Bulk
nutrition_rules: Carbs 4-7 g/kg higher on training days
Body comp check every 4-6 weeks (if >60% fat gain, reduce surplus)
rationale: Muscle gain phase with controlled surplus, high protein distribution, and body composition monitoring to minimize fat gain.

title: Fat Loss with Muscle Preservation
nutrition_rules: Carbs around training, reduce rest days
Duration 8-16 weeks max then 4+ weeks maintenance
rationale: Evidence-based cutting protocol prioritizing muscle preservation through elevated protein, maintained intensity, and strategic refeeds.

title: General Weight Loss
nutrition_rules: Portion awareness, protein each meal, hydration
NO 'burning off meals' or 'earning food' language
rationale: Sustainable weight loss approach emphasizing consistency, resistance training, adequate protein, and healthy relationship with food.

title: Maintenance / General Health
nutrition_rules: Monitor body composition not just scale
Higher-load resistance training superior for recomp
rationale: Maintenance and recomposition phase focusing on body composition improvement at maintenance calories with progressive overload.

title: Performance Goal
nutrition_rules: No deficit during peak training blocks
rationale: Performance-focused goal prioritizing event-specific periodization and adequate fueling over body composition goals.

title: Strength Nutrition by Goal
nutrition_rules: Bulk: +10-20% TDEE, protein 1.6-2.2 g/kg, carbs 4-7 g/kg, fat 0.5-1.5 g/kg
Cut: -15-25% TDEE, protein 1.6-2.4 g/kg, carbs 2-4 g/kg, fat 0.5-1.0 g/kg, refeed 1-2x/week
Weight loss: -20-30% TDEE, protein 1.4-2.0, carbs 2-4, fat 0.5-1.0
rationale: Goal-specific macro targets for strength athletes across bulk, cut, maintenance, and weight loss phases.

title: Endurance Nutrition by Distance
nutrition_rules: 5K/10K: carbs 3-5 g/kg, protein 1.4-1.8, no carb loading, no during-race fuel needed
Half marathon: carbs 5-7, protein 1.4-1.8, 24hr carb load 7-8 g/kg, gel every 45-60min >75min, 150-300ml/20min
Marathon: carbs 7-10 peak, protein 1.4-2.0, carb load 24-48hr 8-12 g/kg, 60-90g carbs/hr from first 15min, sodium 500-700mg/hr
Ultra: carbs 8-12 peak, protein 1.4-2.0, 48hr carb load, 150-400 kcal/hr real food OK, sodium+electrolytes
rationale: Distance-specific nutrition protocols scaling carbohydrate needs, race fueling, and hydration strategies from 5K through ultra distances.

title: Cycling and Triathlon Nutrition
nutrition_rules: Cycling: carbs 3-5 easy days, 8-12 hard days
Triathlon: 73% Ironman DNFs trace to nutrition
rationale: Cycling and triathlon-specific nutrition emphasizing carb periodization by training day intensity and critical race-day fueling protocols.

title: HIIT and Bodyweight Nutrition
nutrition_rules: Pre-HIIT: fast carbs 60-90min before (fasted HIIT no advantage)
rationale: HIIT and bodyweight training nutrition with pre/post timing protocols and daily macro targets.

title: Yoga, Mobility, and Walking Nutrition
nutrition_rules: Lower intensity does NOT mean low protein
rationale: Nutrition for lower-intensity modalities emphasizing that protein needs remain substantial regardless of training intensity.

title: Universal Hydration Guidelines
nutrition_rules: Endurance events: sport-specific protocols by duration/environment/sweat rate
rationale: Evidence-based hydration approach favoring thirst-driven intake over rigid formulas, with sport-specific adjustments for endurance events.

title: Exercise Rotation Protocol
plan_rules: Tier 1 primary compounds: stable 4-6 weeks per mesocycle (squat, bench, deadlift, OHP, row)
Tier 2 secondary: rotate every 2-4 weeks (incline bench, DB press, front squat, RDL, cable row)
Tier 3 accessories: rotate every 1-3 weeks (lateral raises, curls, tricep work)
Between mesocycles: swap Tier 2 and all Tier 3
rationale: Tiered exercise rotation balancing movement stability for progression tracking with sufficient variation to prevent accommodation.

title: Rep Range Undulation
rationale: Weekly rep range undulation within a mesocycle targeting different training stimuli while managing fatigue through a built-in deload.

title: Running Workout Variation
plan_rules: Each week different workout types, not same run repeated
Easy runs (Z2) 2-3 days (similar week to week)
Quality 1: varies by phase (base=steady-state, build=tempo/threshold, peak=race-pace)
Long run: Week 1 all easy, Week 2 last 3mi tempo, Week 3 progression, Week 4 cutback easy
rationale: Running variation protocol ensuring weekly workout diversity while maintaining consistent easy run volume and phase-appropriate quality sessions.

title: HIIT Workout Variation
plan_rules: Rotate formats: circuit, Tabata, EMOM, AMRAP
Rotate focus: full body, upper, lower, core, cardio-heavy
Rotate equipment: bodyweight, dumbbells, kettlebell, full gym
rationale: HIIT variation across format, focus area, equipment, and work:rest ratios to prevent staleness and drive adaptation.

title: Mesocycle and Macrocycle Structure
plan_rules: Mesocycle (4 weeks): Wk1 intro/moderate below target, Wk2 build at target progressing, Wk3 peak highest volume/intensity, Wk4 deload -40-50% volume
Macrocycle (12-16 weeks): Block 1 (1-4) hypertrophy, Block 2 (5-8) strength, Block 3 (9-12) peak/power, Block 4 (13-16) active recovery/transition
Between blocks: swap Tier 2+3 exercises, adjust rep schemes, potentially swap split
rationale: Structured mesocycle and macrocycle periodization providing systematic volume/intensity progression with built-in recovery and phase transitions.

title: Safety Boundaries and Constraints
hard_constraints: No prohibited phrases (guaranteed results, lose X in Y days, burn off meal, cure, treat, diagnose)
Injury escalation: persistent pain -> professional referral
rationale: Safety and sustainability outrank optimization. These are non-negotiable.

title: Offseason / transition phase for runners
principles: Offseason volume: 50-60% of peak training volume
Sessions should be shorter (30-45 min most runs)
Focus shifts to speed development, hill work, and strength training
2-3 strength sessions/week — best time in the annual cycle to prioritize gym work
Encourage enjoyment and exploration — reduce watch dependency
plan_rules: Reduce weekly mileage to 50-60% of the user's peak training volume
Include 2-3 stride sessions per week (6-10 x 80-100m near-sprint with full recovery)
Include 1 hill session per week (6-10 x 60-90s hard effort hill repeats)
Include 2-3 strength training sessions targeting compound lower body + single-leg work
No structured interval or tempo work — keep running aerobic and fun
Duration: 2-6 weeks depending on preceding training block intensity and race
hard_constraints: Never reduce volume below 40% of peak (risk of excessive detraining)
Never eliminate running entirely unless medically indicated
Strength training intensity can increase but running intensity should decrease
coaching_tone: Relaxed, encouraging exploration. Less pressure, more autonomy. 'This is your time to run without a watch.'
evidence_sources: Daniels — Daniels' Running Formula (transition phase guidance)
General coaching consensus on offseason volume reduction
rationale: The offseason allows physical and mental recovery while maintaining a fitness base. Speed and strength work during reduced volume builds qualities that are hard to develop during high-volume training blocks.

title: Tiered heart rate zone calculation system
principles: Progressive accuracy: zones improve as user provides more data
Tier 1 (age only): Max HR = 208 - 0.7*age. Standard error +/-10 bpm.
Tier 2 (age + resting HR): Karvonen formula. Accounts for individual fitness level.
Tier 3 (LTHR): Zones as % of lactate threshold HR. Most accurate for trained runners.
Always use the highest tier available. If user provides known max HR, use it instead of age-predicted.
plan_rules: Use highest available zone tier when prescribing intensity
When resting HR is added, recalculate all zones and notify user of upgrade
Complement HR zones with pace zones from VDOT for cross-reference
hard_constraints: Never prescribe zones without at least age data
Display accuracy note for Tier 1 zones (age-estimated)
evidence_sources: Tanaka et al. (2001) — Age-predicted maximal heart rate revisited, JACC
Karvonen et al. (1957) — The effects of training on heart rate
ACSM Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription, 11th ed.
rationale: Progressive accuracy: zones improve as user provides more data. No user is blocked by lack of lab testing, but serious runners get precise zones when they have the data.

title: 1/3 feedback calibration rule for plan adjustment
principles: Post-hoc review tool, not a training prescription
~1/3 workouts should feel easier, ~1/3 about right, ~1/3 harder than intended
Assesses perceived effort relative to intent, NOT intensity distribution
Compatible with 80/20 polarized model — both frameworks coexist
plan_rules: After each completed workout, prompt: How did this feel compared to what you expected? (Easier / About right / Harder)
Aggregate over the week and surface calibration signals in weekly check-in
If >50% of workouts feel harder than intended for 2+ consecutive weeks: reduce volume 10% or intensity one notch
If >50% of workouts feel easier than intended for 2+ consecutive weeks: consider progression step
Single-week deviations are normal and should not trigger automatic adjustments
hard_constraints: This rule must never override Tier 1 evidence-based training prescriptions
Framed as feedback tool only — never as a training distribution target
evidence_sources: Compatible with Seiler (2010) polarized training model (Tier 1)
rationale: Provides a simple, user-friendly feedback mechanism that helps the app (and user) detect when training load is miscalibrated. Framed as a review tool, not a prescription, so it doesn't conflict with evidence-based programming rules.
